mission.title: Install and Configure DigitalOcean Kubernetes (DOKS)
mission.description: DigitalOcean Kubernetes (DOKS) is a managed Kubernetes service designed for simplicity and cost-effectiveness in container orchestration. It is ideal for developers and teams looking to deploy and manage Kubernetes clusters without the overhead of managing the underlying infrastructure.

steps.title: Create a DOKS Cluster
steps.description:
Use the DigitalOcean CLI (doctl) to create a Kubernetes cluster. Replace `<cluster-name>`, `<region>`, and `<node-count>` with your desired values.

```bash
# Install doctl if you haven't already
brew install doctl

# Authenticate with your DigitalOcean account
doctl auth init

# Create the Kubernetes cluster
doctl k8s cluster create <cluster-name> --region <region> --node-pool "name=default;size=s-2vcpu-4gb;count=<node-count>"
```

**Note:** Ensure you select a region that supports your desired Kubernetes version.

steps.title: Connect kubectl to the Cluster
steps.description:
Once the cluster is created, configure kubectl to connect to it:

```bash
# Get the kubeconfig for your cluster
doctl k8s cluster kubeconfig save <cluster-name>

# Verify kubectl is connected
gkubectl cluster-info
```

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Set up Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) for your deployments:

```bash
# Create a deployment (example)
kubectl create deployment my-app --image=nginx

# Expose the deployment
gkubectl expose deployment my-app --port=80 --target-port=80

# Enable HPA
gkubectl autoscale deployment my-app --cpu-percent=50 --min=1 --max=10
```
Verify HPA is working:
```bash
kubectl get hpa
```

uninstall.title: Delete the DOKS Cluster
uninstall.description:
To uninstall the DOKS cluster, run:

```bash
doctl k8s cluster delete <cluster-name>
```
This command will remove the cluster and all associated resources. Ensure you have backed up any necessary data before proceeding.

upgrade.title: Upgrade DOKS Cluster
upgrade.description:
To upgrade your DOKS cluster to a newer version, follow these steps:
1. Check the current version:
   ```bash
   doctl k8s cluster get <cluster-name>
   ```
2. Upgrade the cluster:
   ```bash
   doctl k8s cluster upgrade <cluster-name> --version <new-version>
   ```
3. Verify the upgrade:
   ```bash
   kubectl get nodes
   ```
4. Backup your data before upgrading, especially if you are moving between major versions. Use tools like Velero for backup.
5. If issues arise, you can roll back to the previous version using the same upgrade command with the old version number.

troubleshooting.title: kubectl not connecting to the cluster
troubleshooting.description:
If kubectl cannot connect, ensure your kubeconfig is set up correctly. Run:
```bash
cat ~/.kube/config
```
Check for the correct context and cluster name. If incorrect, re-run the kubeconfig command:
doctl k8s cluster kubeconfig save <cluster-name>.

troubleshooting.title: Cluster status is 'Pending'
troubleshooting.description:
If your cluster is stuck in 'Pending', check for resource availability in your selected region. Use:
```bash
doctl k8s cluster get <cluster-name>
```
If resources are insufficient, consider changing the region or adjusting node pool sizes.

troubleshooting.title: HPA not scaling
troubleshooting.description:
If HPA is not scaling as expected, ensure your application is consuming CPU resources. Check with:
```bash
kubectl top pods
```
If no metrics are reported, ensure Metrics Server is installed:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml
```

versionNotes.changes: Improved performance and stability with enhanced autoscaling features.
Introduced new node pool configurations and improved security features.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated legacy API versions for certain resources.

resolution.summary: A successful setup includes a running DOKS cluster with kubectl configured, and the ability to deploy applications with autoscaling enabled. Verify by checking the cluster status and deployed applications.
codeSnippets:
# Install doctl if you haven't already
brew install doctl

# Authenticate with your DigitalOcean account
doctl auth init

# Create the Kubernetes cluster
doctl k8s cluster create <cluster-name> --region <region> --node-pool "name=default;size=s-2vcpu-4gb;count=<node-count>"

# Get the kubeconfig for your cluster
doctl k8s cluster kubeconfig save <cluster-name>

# Verify kubectl is connected
gkubectl cluster-info

# Create a deployment (example)
kubectl create deployment my-app --image=nginx

# Expose the deployment
gkubectl expose deployment my-app --port=80 --target-port=80

# Enable HPA
gkubectl autoscale deployment my-app --cpu-percent=50 --min=1 --max=10

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have an active DigitalOcean account and the doctl CLI installed.
